subtopics.title: Reading and Comprehension Techniques
common_misconceptions: Reading word by word instead of in chunks
Not reading questions before the passage
Spending too much time on one question
subtopics.teaching_note: Grammar rule: Train students to read the questions first before the passage. This helps them know what to look for. Skim for main ideas (30 seconds), then scan for specific details. Practice with WAEC past questions regularly.

subtopics.title: Types of Comprehension Answers
common_misconceptions: Copying directly from passage without paraphrasing
Adding personal opinions not in passage
Not using complete sentences for answers
subtopics.teaching_note: Grammar rule: Answers must be in complete sentences unless otherwise stated. Never copy directly from the passage - paraphrase using your own words.

subtopics.title: Summary Writing Techniques
common_misconceptions: Including examples and illustrations in summary
subtopics.teaching_note: Grammar rule: A summary must be in continuous prose (paragraphs), NOT point form. Use connectives: firstly, moreover, consequently, finally. Count words carefully - WAEC usually specifies 150 words.

subtopics.title: Selecting Relevant Points
common_misconceptions: Including all points regardless of relevance
subtopics.teaching_note: Rule: Examples, illustrations, and repetitions are NOT main points. If the question asks for numbered points, number them clearly. Read the question carefully - it may specify how many points to include.

subtopics.title: Types of Essays
subtopics.teaching_note: Grammar rule: Every essay must have an introduction, body paragraphs, and conclusion. Plan for 5 minutes before writing. Aim for at least 450 words. Use paragraphs - each main idea gets its own paragraph.

subtopics.title: Formal Letter Writing
common_misconceptions: Using 'Dear Sir' without addressing a specific person
Writing 'Yours faithfully' when using a name
subtopics.teaching_note: Format rule: If salutation is 'Dear Sir/Madam', use 'Yours faithfully'. If salutation uses a name (Dear Mr. Ade), use 'Yours sincerely'. Always include writer's address at top right and date.

subtopics.title: Speech Writing
common_misconceptions: Writing like an essay without addressing the audience
Using 'Dear Sir' instead of addressing the audience directly
subtopics.teaching_note: Format: Start with title (e.g., 'A WELCOME ADDRESS'), then salutation (The Principal, staff, and fellow students...). Use direct address: 'I stand before you today...'. End with 'Thank you'.

subtopics.title: Synonyms and Antonyms
common_misconceptions: Choosing a synonym without considering context
Not reading the full sentence for context
subtopics.teaching_note: Grammar rule: Always consider the CONTEXT when choosing synonyms. 'Bright' can mean intelligent (bright student) or shining (bright light) - context determines meaning.

subtopics.title: Word Formation and Vocabulary Development
subtopics.teaching_note: Common prefixes: un-, re-, dis-, mis-, pre-, over-. Common suffixes: -tion (noun), -ful (adjective), -ly (adverb), -ment (noun). Root 'spect' = see (inspect, respect, spectator).

subtopics.title: Parts of Speech
subtopics.teaching_note: Grammar rules: Adjectives describe nouns (beautiful girl). Adverbs describe verbs (run quickly). A word can be different parts of speech depending on usage: 'book' the noun vs 'book' the verb.

subtopics.title: Tenses and Verb Forms
common_misconceptions: Using present continuous for habitual actions
Confusing past simple with present perfect
Subject-verb agreement errors with collective nouns
subtopics.teaching_note: Grammar rules: Present simple for habits (I eat rice daily). Present continuous for now (I am eating now). Present perfect for unfinished time (I have eaten today). Past simple for completed actions (I ate yesterday).

subtopics.title: Sentence Structure
common_misconceptions: Writing sentence fragments instead of complete sentences
Comma splices (joining two independent clauses with just a comma)
subtopics.teaching_note: Grammar rules: A simple sentence has one main clause. A compound sentence has two+ main clauses joined by coordinating conjunctions (FANBOYS: for, and, nor, but, or, yet, so). A complex sentence has one main clause and one+ subordinate clauses.

subtopics.title: Vowels and Consonants
common_misconceptions: Confusing vowel sounds with vowel letters
subtopics.teaching_note: Pronunciation guide: English has 20 vowel sounds (12 pure + 8 diphthongs) but only 5 vowel letters. Pure vowels: /iː/ (see), /ɪ/ (sit), /e/ (bed), /æ/ (cat), /ɑː/ (car), /ɒ/ (hot), /ɔː/ (law), /ʊ/ (put), /uː/ (food), /ʌ/ (cup), /ɜː/ (bird), /ə/ (about).

subtopics.title: Stress and Rhythm
common_misconceptions: Not understanding that stress can change word meaning
subtopics.teaching_note: Stress rule: Two-syllable nouns are usually stressed on the first syllable (TAble, WAter). Two-syllable verbs are usually stressed on the second syllable (beGIN, aRRIVE). JAMB frequently tests word stress.

subtopics.title: Rhyme, Alliteration and Assonance
common_misconceptions: Thinking rhymes must be spelled similarly
subtopics.teaching_note: Definitions: Alliteration = repeated consonant sounds at word beginnings (Peter Piper). Assonance = repeated vowel sounds (sld owly). Rhyme = same ending sounds (cat/hat). Rhyme scheme is marked with letters (ABAB).
